items.q: What is the formula for Young's modulus?
items.correct: Stress / strain — the ratio in the linear (elastic) region of a stress-strain graph.
items.misconception: Force / area — the same as stress itself.
items.random_wrong: Extension / original length — the same as strain.
items.why: Students remember the component words (stress, strain) but mix up that Young's modulus is their ratio, not one of them alone.

items.q: When two waves interfere destructively, what happens to the energy?
items.correct: Energy is redistributed — there is constructive interference elsewhere, conserving total energy.
items.misconception: Energy is destroyed at points of destructive interference.
items.random_wrong: Energy turns into mass according to E=mc^2 when waves cancel.
items.why: Students take 'destructive' literally and assume energy is annihilated, missing that interference always involves a redistribution pattern.

items.q: If you double your distance from a point source of waves, what happens to the intensity?
items.correct: It falls to one quarter — intensity follows an inverse-square law with distance from a point source.
items.misconception: It halves — intensity is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged because the wave doesn't lose energy.
items.why: Students default to linear scaling instead of the inverse-square law that arises because the same energy spreads over a sphere of growing surface area.

items.q: For Young's slits, fringe spacing w equals:
items.why: It's lambda D / s. “s D / lambda” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Constructive interference requires path difference of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: n lambda. “(n+0.5) lambda” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: lambda = 600 nm, s = 0.3 mm, D = 1.5 m. Fringe spacing in mm?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
